romania.title: O ginecoloagă din Iași își îndeamnă pacientele, pe Facebook, să nu conceapă copii în post: „Sarcini oprite. Sau, după ce se naște, copilul moare în pătuț” / „Nu văd care este problema în a da un sfat din suflet”
romania.summary: Cristina Buzgar, medic ginecolog la Iași, afirmă, într-un mesaj video postat online, că „a observat, statistic vorbind”, că, în cazul copiilor concepuți în perioada posturilor religioase, există „mai multe sarcini oprite din evoluție, sau care, pe parcurs, dezvoltă anomalii, boli. Sau, după ce se naște copilul, pățește ceva suspect – moare în pătuț acasă, capătă …

romania.summary: Moţiunea de cenzură AUR-PSD a fost depusă astăzi în Parlament, dar încă nu putem fi siguri dacă e valabilă! Curtea Constituţională e aşteptată să se pronunţe după ce s-a constatat că multe dintre semnăturile ataşate nu sunt chiar semnături propriu-zise, ci amprente ale degetului mare şi X-uri. „E o situaţie neobişnuită: avem 90 de iscălituri, […] The post Moţiunea de cenzură AUR-PSD, depusă cu 90 de semnături, 112 amprente şi 33 de X-uri appeared first on Times New Roman .

În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

medical.title: Exams: How to use exercise to boost your revision
medical.summary: It's revision season. If you're a student preparing for upcoming exams, you might be tempted to put aside sport or other physical activity for a while in order to dedicate more time to learning.

medical.title: Teenage girls and experts call for changes to tackle worsening mental health
medical.summary: Researchers from The University of Manchester have worked directly with teenage girls to uncover what they believe could help turn the tide on rising rates of anxiety and low mood. From their early teenage years, girls are more likely to experience low mood and anxiety. They are twice as likely as boys to experience depression by age 15, and new evidence suggests this gap has been growing for more than a decade.

medical.title: Team targets the spinal cord to solve paralysis' most overlooked problem
medical.summary: Approximately 308,000 people in the United States live with spinal cord injury. Nearly all lose bladder control. And yet the vast majority of research and engineering attention in neurotech has poured into motor restoration—making paralyzed limbs move again.

medical.title: From fear to trust: How music is transforming dental care
medical.summary: Treating patients who have spent years—sometimes decades—avoiding dental care because of fear, disability, trauma or painful past experiences can be challenging. "They are afraid and don't want to be treated," said Adela Planerova, DDS, MS, chair Special Needs Dentistry at University of Rochester Medicine Eastman Institute for Oral Health. "Some are even afraid to enter the room. When they eventually sit in the chair, they often try to push the provider away. Many of our patients move constantly, while others have persistent involuntary movements because of their condition, such as cerebral palsy."

medical.title: A gene that keeps intestinal stem cells stable offers insight into how tissues repair themselves
medical.summary: Years before he conducted the research that would earn him a Nobel Prize in Physiology and Medicine, Shinya Yamanaka, MD, Ph.D., was a postdoctoral scientist at Gladstone Institutes, studying genes. There, he helped discover a gene (now called eIF4G2) that's essential for early embryonic development.

medical.title: Years before pregnancy, routine bloodwork may already signal which women will face one of its riskiest complications
medical.summary: Small abnormalities in blood sugar, blood lipids and inflammation several years before pregnancy are linked to an increased risk of high blood pressure during pregnancy and preeclampsia, according to a study from Karolinska Institutet published in JAMA Network Open. The findings may contribute to earlier risk assessment and preventive measures in antenatal care.

medical.title: For 30 years, doctors chased the wrong immune culprit behind this rare inflammatory disease
medical.summary: Researchers at the Garvan Institute of Medical Research have uncovered a critical mechanism driving inflammation in mevalonate kinase deficiency (MKD), a rare but devastating autoinflammatory disorder. The study, published in Immunity, reveals that immune cells called natural killer (NK) cells are impaired in MKD, leading to an excessive inflammatory response when patients encounter infections.

medical.title: Why this CAR T advance matters: Complete remissions without chemotherapy at doses as low as 250,000 cells/kg
medical.summary: Stem-cell memory T (TSCM) cells are a rare subset of immune cells with the ability to self-renew, persist long term, and mount potent anti-tumor responses. These properties make them an attractive candidate for next-generation CAR T-cell therapies. However, their clinical potential has not previously been demonstrated in humans.

medical.title: Disease-causing pathogen rewires gut metabolism to secure nutrients for growth, research shows
medical.summary: An intestinal pathogen reshapes the gut environment to fuel its own colonization and cause diseases, a multi-institutional team including researchers at Vanderbilt Health has discovered. The investigators show that enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis (ETBF), which causes diarrhea and has been implicated in colitis (inflammation of the colon) and colorectal cancer, uses a toxin it produces to reprogram intestinal cell metabolism and generate conditions that support its growth.

medical.title: Boosting incomes for the poorest families could reduce child protection plans, study finds
medical.summary: New research led by Kingston University has found that even very modest uplifts to family finances could reduce children's chances of entering the child protection system. The study, based on data from six local authorities, shows that children living in the poorest households, where the families were below the poverty line and expenditure often exceeded their scarce income, were more likely to experience repeat involvement with children's social care services and to be placed on child protection plans than those in slightly better off families.

medical.title: The surprising reason you’re so productive one day and not the next
medical.summary: Feeling mentally “on” isn’t just in your head—it can significantly boost what you accomplish. Researchers found that sharper thinking on a given day leads people to set bigger goals and actually follow through. That edge can equal up to 40 extra minutes of productivity. But push too hard for too long, and the effect reverses.

science.title: Hidden in the headlines: New study uses local news to assess wildlife poaching in Romania
science.summary: Graceful, brown-eyed, and a staple of local folklore, the roe deer is one of Romania's most iconic forest dwellers. But behind the serene image of these animals lies a hidden crisis: a new study reveals the roe deer is the most frequently poached mammal in the country, a finding made possible by turning to an unlikely source of scientific data: the local news.

science.title: A silent robot shadows sperm whales by listening to their clicks
science.summary: An autonomous underwater glider is giving us a new and effective way to track sperm whales by tuning into their clicks and silently following them. To study these large oceanic predators, researchers need to monitor their movements and social interactions for months at a time. But that's not easy, because they swim deep and stay underwater for long periods, making them hard to reach.

science.title: Lithium in the Appalachians could replace imports for a century or more, estimates suggest
science.summary: The southern Appalachians hold an estimated 1.43 million metric tons of lithium oxide, concentrated in the Carolinas, and the northern Appalachians hold an estimated 900,000 metric tons, concentrated in Maine and New Hampshire, according to estimates in a new USGS scientific paper published in Natural Resources Research. The lithium is present in pegmatites, large-grained rocks similar to granite.

science.title: Researchers create DNA 'nano-rings' to control viral cell proteins
science.summary: Scientists at Durham University, working in partnership with Jagiellonian University in Poland, have developed a new nanoscale tool that can capture and precisely position some of the most important proteins in the human body, opening up new possibilities for medicine, imaging, and bioengineering.

science.title: European rocket puts Amazon internet satellites in orbit
science.summary: Europe's most powerful rocket Ariane 6 successfully released 32 satellites into orbit Thursday for Amazon's internet constellation, which is bidding to rival Elon Musk's giant Starlink.

science.title: XXP instrument back online, marking a key milestone in high-energy upgrade to SLAC's X-ray laser
science.summary: XPP, the X-ray Pump Probe instrument at the Linac Coherent Light Source (LCLS), is back online and welcoming researchers after a complete rebuild. The overhaul has readied XPP for the significant increase in X-ray output expected from the ongoing high-energy upgrade to LCLS at the Department of Energy's SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory. LCLS is a pioneering X-ray free-electron laser facility used by scientists around the world to capture ultrafast snapshots of natural processes.

science.title: Room-temperature multiferroic could pave way to low-energy computing
science.summary: A team of researchers at Rice University has engineered a new version of a well-known multiferroic that exhibits orders of magnitude higher performance at room temperature than its parent material. The study, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, describes a modified version of bismuth ferrite that shows a 10-fold increase in magnetization and 100-fold increase in magnetoelectric coupling compared to standard varieties.

science.title: Rap fans share the bad rap in court
science.summary: An innovative James Cook University experiment has found rap-related cues shape how people judge a hypothetical fan accused of violent crime, raising questions about the use of lyrics as evidence in court. James Cook University psychology senior lecturer Amanda Krause, who coauthored the study published in the Psychology of Music, said almost half of the top 50 most-streamed artists are classified as hip-hop or rap.

science.title: Perceptions of cultural foreignness may lead to job discrimination, study finds
science.summary: Asian, Arab and Latino Americans may face more discrimination when seeking jobs that emphasize stereotypically American characteristics because they are deemed to be culturally foreign, according to research published in the Journal of Experimental Psychology

science.title: New study finds urban parks in less privileged neighborhoods are smaller, hotter and more polluted
science.summary: A new study from researchers at George Washington University Milken Institute of Public Health reveals that not all urban parks provide equal health benefits. Parks in less privileged neighborhoods across the United States tend to be smaller, hotter, and more polluted than those in wealthier areas—highlighting persistent environmental inequities in cities nationwide. The work is published in the journal Environmental Research Letters.

science.title: GP Com observations sharpen picture of a rare ultracompact binary system
science.summary: Using the Rozhen National Astronomical Observatory, Bulgarian astronomers have conducted optical photometric observations of an ultracompact binary known as GP Com. Results of the observational campaign, presented in the Proceedings of the Bulgarian Academy of Sciences, shed more light on the properties of this system.

science.title: ESA's Proba 3 is unlocking secrets of the solar wind
science.summary: It has been a dream of astronomers and solar scientists for ages. A new mission gives solar researchers a powerful new tool in their arsenal: on-demand, total solar eclipses. Launched in 2024, The European Space Agency's Proba-3 mission has proven the feasibility of a free-flying, space-based coronagraph. Now, the first science results from the mission are giving us a view of the origin of space weather. The results were recently published in The Astrophysical Journal Letters.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: 😂 Moţiunea de cenzură AUR-PSD, depusă cu 90 de semnături, 112 amprente şi 33 de X-uri
mix_items.summary: Moţiunea de cenzură AUR-PSD a fost depusă astăzi în Parlament, dar încă nu putem fi siguri dacă e valabilă! Curtea Constituţională e aşteptată să se pronunţe după ce s-a constatat că multe dintre semnăturile ataşate nu sunt chiar semnături propriu-zise, ci amprente ale degetului mare şi X-uri. „E o situaţie neobişnuită: avem 90 de iscălituri, […] The post Moţiunea de cenzură AUR-PSD, depusă cu 90 de semnături, 112 amprente şi 33 de X-uri appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: O ginecoloagă din Iași își îndeamnă pacientele, pe Facebook, să nu conceapă copii în post: „Sarcini oprite. Sau, după ce se naște, copilul moare în pătuț” / „Nu văd care este problema în a da un sfat din suflet”
mix_items.summary: Cristina Buzgar, medic ginecolog la Iași, afirmă, într-un mesaj video postat online, că „a observat, statistic vorbind”, că, în cazul copiilor concepuți în perioada posturilor religioase, există „mai multe sarcini oprite din evoluție, sau care, pe parcurs, dezvoltă anomalii, boli. Sau, după ce se naște copilul, pățește ceva suspect – moare în pătuț acasă, capătă …

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Exams: How to use exercise to boost your revision
mix_items.summary: It's revision season. If you're a student preparing for upcoming exams, you might be tempted to put aside sport or other physical activity for a while in order to dedicate more time to learning.

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Teenage girls and experts call for changes to tackle worsening mental health
mix_items.summary: Researchers from The University of Manchester have worked directly with teenage girls to uncover what they believe could help turn the tide on rising rates of anxiety and low mood. From their early teenage years, girls are more likely to experience low mood and anxiety. They are twice as likely as boys to experience depression by age 15, and new evidence suggests this gap has been growing for more than a decade.

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Team targets the spinal cord to solve paralysis' most overlooked problem
mix_items.summary: Approximately 308,000 people in the United States live with spinal cord injury. Nearly all lose bladder control. And yet the vast majority of research and engineering attention in neurotech has poured into motor restoration—making paralyzed limbs move again.

mix_items.title: From fear to trust: How music is transforming dental care
mix_items.summary: Treating patients who have spent years—sometimes decades—avoiding dental care because of fear, disability, trauma or painful past experiences can be challenging. "They are afraid and don't want to be treated," said Adela Planerova, DDS, MS, chair Special Needs Dentistry at University of Rochester Medicine Eastman Institute for Oral Health. "Some are even afraid to enter the room. When they eventually sit in the chair, they often try to push the provider away. Many of our patients move constantly, while others have persistent involuntary movements because of their condition, such as cerebral palsy."

mix_items.title: A gene that keeps intestinal stem cells stable offers insight into how tissues repair themselves
mix_items.summary: Years before he conducted the research that would earn him a Nobel Prize in Physiology and Medicine, Shinya Yamanaka, MD, Ph.D., was a postdoctoral scientist at Gladstone Institutes, studying genes. There, he helped discover a gene (now called eIF4G2) that's essential for early embryonic development.

mix_items.title: Years before pregnancy, routine bloodwork may already signal which women will face one of its riskiest complications
mix_items.summary: Small abnormalities in blood sugar, blood lipids and inflammation several years before pregnancy are linked to an increased risk of high blood pressure during pregnancy and preeclampsia, according to a study from Karolinska Institutet published in JAMA Network Open. The findings may contribute to earlier risk assessment and preventive measures in antenatal care.

mix_items.title: For 30 years, doctors chased the wrong immune culprit behind this rare inflammatory disease
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Garvan Institute of Medical Research have uncovered a critical mechanism driving inflammation in mevalonate kinase deficiency (MKD), a rare but devastating autoinflammatory disorder. The study, published in Immunity, reveals that immune cells called natural killer (NK) cells are impaired in MKD, leading to an excessive inflammatory response when patients encounter infections.

mix_items.title: Why this CAR T advance matters: Complete remissions without chemotherapy at doses as low as 250,000 cells/kg
mix_items.summary: Stem-cell memory T (TSCM) cells are a rare subset of immune cells with the ability to self-renew, persist long term, and mount potent anti-tumor responses. These properties make them an attractive candidate for next-generation CAR T-cell therapies. However, their clinical potential has not previously been demonstrated in humans.

mix_items.title: Disease-causing pathogen rewires gut metabolism to secure nutrients for growth, research shows
mix_items.summary: An intestinal pathogen reshapes the gut environment to fuel its own colonization and cause diseases, a multi-institutional team including researchers at Vanderbilt Health has discovered. The investigators show that enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis (ETBF), which causes diarrhea and has been implicated in colitis (inflammation of the colon) and colorectal cancer, uses a toxin it produces to reprogram intestinal cell metabolism and generate conditions that support its growth.

mix_items.title: Boosting incomes for the poorest families could reduce child protection plans, study finds
mix_items.summary: New research led by Kingston University has found that even very modest uplifts to family finances could reduce children's chances of entering the child protection system. The study, based on data from six local authorities, shows that children living in the poorest households, where the families were below the poverty line and expenditure often exceeded their scarce income, were more likely to experience repeat involvement with children's social care services and to be placed on child protection plans than those in slightly better off families.

mix_items.title: Hidden in the headlines: New study uses local news to assess wildlife poaching in Romania
mix_items.summary: Graceful, brown-eyed, and a staple of local folklore, the roe deer is one of Romania's most iconic forest dwellers. But behind the serene image of these animals lies a hidden crisis: a new study reveals the roe deer is the most frequently poached mammal in the country, a finding made possible by turning to an unlikely source of scientific data: the local news.

mix_items.title: A silent robot shadows sperm whales by listening to their clicks
mix_items.summary: An autonomous underwater glider is giving us a new and effective way to track sperm whales by tuning into their clicks and silently following them. To study these large oceanic predators, researchers need to monitor their movements and social interactions for months at a time. But that's not easy, because they swim deep and stay underwater for long periods, making them hard to reach.

mix_items.title: Lithium in the Appalachians could replace imports for a century or more, estimates suggest
mix_items.summary: The southern Appalachians hold an estimated 1.43 million metric tons of lithium oxide, concentrated in the Carolinas, and the northern Appalachians hold an estimated 900,000 metric tons, concentrated in Maine and New Hampshire, according to estimates in a new USGS scientific paper published in Natural Resources Research. The lithium is present in pegmatites, large-grained rocks similar to granite.

mix_items.title: Researchers create DNA 'nano-rings' to control viral cell proteins
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Durham University, working in partnership with Jagiellonian University in Poland, have developed a new nanoscale tool that can capture and precisely position some of the most important proteins in the human body, opening up new possibilities for medicine, imaging, and bioengineering.

mix_items.title: European rocket puts Amazon internet satellites in orbit
mix_items.summary: Europe's most powerful rocket Ariane 6 successfully released 32 satellites into orbit Thursday for Amazon's internet constellation, which is bidding to rival Elon Musk's giant Starlink.

mix_items.title: XXP instrument back online, marking a key milestone in high-energy upgrade to SLAC's X-ray laser
mix_items.summary: XPP, the X-ray Pump Probe instrument at the Linac Coherent Light Source (LCLS), is back online and welcoming researchers after a complete rebuild. The overhaul has readied XPP for the significant increase in X-ray output expected from the ongoing high-energy upgrade to LCLS at the Department of Energy's SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory. LCLS is a pioneering X-ray free-electron laser facility used by scientists around the world to capture ultrafast snapshots of natural processes.

mix_items.title: Room-temperature multiferroic could pave way to low-energy computing
mix_items.summary: A team of researchers at Rice University has engineered a new version of a well-known multiferroic that exhibits orders of magnitude higher performance at room temperature than its parent material. The study, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, describes a modified version of bismuth ferrite that shows a 10-fold increase in magnetization and 100-fold increase in magnetoelectric coupling compared to standard varieties.

mix_items.title: Rap fans share the bad rap in court
mix_items.summary: An innovative James Cook University experiment has found rap-related cues shape how people judge a hypothetical fan accused of violent crime, raising questions about the use of lyrics as evidence in court. James Cook University psychology senior lecturer Amanda Krause, who coauthored the study published in the Psychology of Music, said almost half of the top 50 most-streamed artists are classified as hip-hop or rap.

mix_items.title: Perceptions of cultural foreignness may lead to job discrimination, study finds
mix_items.summary: Asian, Arab and Latino Americans may face more discrimination when seeking jobs that emphasize stereotypically American characteristics because they are deemed to be culturally foreign, according to research published in the Journal of Experimental Psychology

mix_items.title: New study finds urban parks in less privileged neighborhoods are smaller, hotter and more polluted
mix_items.summary: A new study from researchers at George Washington University Milken Institute of Public Health reveals that not all urban parks provide equal health benefits. Parks in less privileged neighborhoods across the United States tend to be smaller, hotter, and more polluted than those in wealthier areas—highlighting persistent environmental inequities in cities nationwide. The work is published in the journal Environmental Research Letters.

mix_items.title: GP Com observations sharpen picture of a rare ultracompact binary system
mix_items.summary: Using the Rozhen National Astronomical Observatory, Bulgarian astronomers have conducted optical photometric observations of an ultracompact binary known as GP Com. Results of the observational campaign, presented in the Proceedings of the Bulgarian Academy of Sciences, shed more light on the properties of this system.

mix_items.title: ESA's Proba 3 is unlocking secrets of the solar wind
mix_items.summary: It has been a dream of astronomers and solar scientists for ages. A new mission gives solar researchers a powerful new tool in their arsenal: on-demand, total solar eclipses. Launched in 2024, The European Space Agency's Proba-3 mission has proven the feasibility of a free-flying, space-based coronagraph. Now, the first science results from the mission are giving us a view of the origin of space weather. The results were recently published in The Astrophysical Journal Letters.

mix_items.title: The surprising reason you’re so productive one day and not the next
mix_items.summary: Feeling mentally “on” isn’t just in your head—it can significantly boost what you accomplish. Researchers found that sharper thinking on a given day leads people to set bigger goals and actually follow through. That edge can equal up to 40 extra minutes of productivity. But push too hard for too long, and the effect reverses.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: M-am dus in bucatarie dupa apa. M-am intors cu sendvis si pace interioara.

satire_ro.title: Soros riscă să-și piardă nemurirea din cauza natalității scăzute: „Găsesc tot mai greu sânge de copil”
